subjects.name: Mathematics
subjects.description: Number, algebra, geometry, statistics and probability
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Mathematics (0580) develops mathematical knowledge and skills as a foundation for further study or practical application. The syllabus covers number concepts, algebra, geometry, trigonometry, statistics, and probability, with candidates entering either Core or Extended tiers. Extended candidates may achieve grades A* to E, while Core candidates achieve C to G. The qualification is assessed through two papers and is essential for progression to A-Level Mathematics and many STEM fields.

topics.title: Number
topics.summary: Fractions, percentages, ratio, standard form, bounds, and directed numbers. Candidates apply numerical skills to solve problems in context.

topics.title: Algebra
topics.summary: Expressions, linear and quadratic equations, inequalities, factorising, formulae, and algebraic fractions. Extended tier includes simultaneous equations and algebraic proof.

topics.title: Geometry
topics.summary: Angles, similarity, congruence, circle theorems, Pythagoras, and properties of polygons. Extended tier covers formal geometric proofs.

topics.title: Trigonometry
topics.summary: Pythagoras theorem, sine and cosine rules, bearings, and three-dimensional trigonometry. Extended candidates solve multi-step problems.

topics.title: Statistics
topics.summary: Averages, cumulative frequency, histograms, scatter diagrams, correlation, and probability trees. Includes data interpretation and analysis.

topics.title: Graphs
topics.summary: Plotting linear and quadratic graphs, gradient and intercept, distance-time and speed-time graphs, and recognising function types.

subjects.name: Physics
subjects.description: Forces, energy, waves, electricity and nuclear physics
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Physics (0625) provides a solid foundation in physical principles and develops practical skills through investigation. The syllabus covers mechanics, thermal physics, waves, electricity and magnetism, and nuclear physics. Candidates are assessed through multiple-choice, theory, and practical or alternative-to-practical papers. Core and Extended tiers allow differentiation, with Extended candidates eligible for grades A* to E. The qualification prepares students for A-Level Physics and engineering pathways.

topics.title: Motion & Forces
topics.summary: Speed, velocity, acceleration, Newton's laws, momentum, impulse, and force diagrams. Includes calculations of resultant forces and motion graphs.

topics.title: Energy
topics.summary: Work, power, energy stores and transfers, kinetic and gravitational potential energy, efficiency, and conservation of energy principles.

topics.title: Waves
topics.summary: Properties of transverse and longitudinal waves, sound, light, reflection, refraction, total internal reflection, lenses, and the electromagnetic spectrum.

topics.title: Electricity
topics.summary: Electric circuits, Ohm's law, series and parallel circuits, power, domestic electricity, and safety including fuses and circuit breakers.

topics.title: Magnetism
topics.summary: Magnetic fields, electromagnets, motor effect, electromagnetic induction, transformers, and the generator effect.

topics.title: Nuclear Physics
topics.summary: Atomic structure, isotopes, radioactive decay, half-life, types of radiation, background radiation, and safety precautions.

topics.title: Thermal Physics
topics.summary: Temperature scales, thermal expansion, specific heat capacity, latent heat, gas laws, and kinetic theory of gases.

subjects.name: Chemistry
subjects.description: Atoms, reactions, organic chemistry and industrial processes
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry (0620) introduces learners to the fundamental principles of chemistry and develops practical investigative skills. The syllabus covers atomic structure, bonding, stoichiometry, acids and bases, salts, electrolysis, organic chemistry, rates of reaction, and industrial processes. Assessment includes multiple-choice, theory, and practical or alternative-to-practical papers. Core and Extended tiers are available, with Extended candidates eligible for grades A* to E. This qualification provides essential preparation for A-Level Chemistry and related sciences.

topics.title: Atoms & Bonding
topics.summary: Atomic structure, protons, neutrons, electrons, isotopes, ionic and covalent bonding, metallic bonding, and giant structures including graphite and diamond.

topics.title: Stoichiometry
topics.summary: Moles, relative atomic mass, empirical and molecular formulae, reacting masses, limiting reagents, and percentage yield calculations.

topics.title: Acids & Bases
topics.summary: pH scale, strong and weak acids, neutralisation, salt preparation methods, titration calculations, and indicators.

topics.title: Electrolysis
topics.summary: Electrolysis of molten and aqueous compounds, products at electrodes, extraction of aluminium, electroplating, and Faraday's laws.

topics.title: Organic Chemistry
topics.summary: Alkanes, alkenes, alcohols, carboxylic acids, polymers, addition and condensation polymerisation, and identification of organic compounds.

topics.title: Rates & Energy
topics.summary: Factors affecting reaction rates, collision theory, activation energy, exothermic and endothermic reactions, and reversible reactions including Haber process.

topics.title: Industrial Chemistry
topics.summary: Haber process, Contact process, fractional distillation of crude oil, cracking, and environmental impacts of industrial processes.

subjects.name: Biology
subjects.description: Cells, organisms, genetics and ecology
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Biology (0610) develops understanding of biological principles and practical skills through investigation. The syllabus covers cell biology, human physiology, plant physiology, genetics, evolution, and ecology. Candidates are assessed through multiple-choice, theory, and practical or alternative-to-practical papers. Core and Extended tiers are available, with Extended candidates eligible for grades A* to E. This qualification provides a strong foundation for A-Level Biology, medicine, and environmental sciences.

topics.title: Cells
topics.summary: Cell structure, prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, specialised cells, diffusion, osmosis, active transport, and cell division by mitosis and meiosis.

topics.title: Human Systems
topics.summary: Digestive system, circulatory system, respiratory system, excretion, coordination by nervous and hormonal systems, and the immune system.

topics.title: Plants
topics.summary: Photosynthesis, leaf structure, transport systems including xylem and phloem, transpiration, and plant hormones including auxins and gibberellins.

topics.title: Genetics
topics.summary: Chromosomes, genes, DNA structure, inheritance patterns, genetic variation, natural selection, evolution, and genetic engineering.

topics.title: Ecology
topics.summary: Food chains and webs, nutrient cycles, population growth, human impact on the environment, conservation, and sustainability.

topics.title: Reproduction
topics.summary: Asexual and sexual reproduction, human reproductive systems, menstrual cycle, fertilisation, contraception, and plant reproduction.

subjects.name: English
subjects.description: Reading, writing, and language analysis skills
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE English Language (0500) develops learners' ability to communicate clearly, accurately, and effectively in both speech and writing. Candidates learn to employ a wide-ranging vocabulary, use correct grammar, spelling, and punctuation, and develop a personal style with an awareness of audience. The syllabus covers reading comprehension, directed writing, narrative and descriptive composition, and language analysis. Assessment includes reading and writing papers, with speaking and listening as optional endorsed components. This qualification is essential for further academic study and professional communication.

topics.title: Reading Comprehension
topics.summary: Explicit and implicit meaning, inference, deduction, summary skills, and analysis of writer's craft including language and structural devices.

topics.title: Directed Writing
topics.summary: Letters, reports, speeches, articles, and reviews written for specific purposes and audiences with appropriate register and tone.

topics.title: Descriptive Writing
topics.summary: Imagery, figurative language, sensory detail, structure, and vocabulary choices to create vivid and engaging descriptions.

topics.title: Narrative Writing
topics.summary: Plot development, characterisation, dialogue, tension, climax, and effective openings and endings for creative stories.

topics.title: Language Analysis
topics.summary: Tone, register, rhetorical devices, figurative language, and how writers achieve specific effects through linguistic choices.

topics.title: Grammar & Punctuation
topics.summary: Sentence structure, punctuation accuracy, spelling, and grammatical conventions to ensure clarity and precision in writing.

subjects.name: History
subjects.description: Modern world history and source analysis
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE History (0470) develops learners' interest in the past and their understanding of historical concepts and skills. The syllabus covers modern world history including the World Wars, the Cold War, international organisations, and depth studies of specific countries or regions. Candidates develop source analysis skills, the ability to evaluate historical evidence, and the capacity to construct coherent historical arguments. Assessment includes source-based questions and structured essays. This qualification prepares students for A-Level History, politics, and international relations.

topics.title: Source Skills
topics.summary: Interpreting, evaluating, and comparing historical sources. Understanding utility, reliability, provenance, and the difference between primary and secondary sources.

topics.title: WWI & WWII
topics.summary: Causes, events, and consequences of the World Wars, including peace treaties, the rise of dictators, and the impact on civilian populations.

topics.title: Cold War
topics.summary: Origins of superpower rivalry, key crises including Berlin and Cuba, nuclear arms race, and the eventual collapse of the Soviet Union.

topics.title: League & UN
topics.summary: International peacekeeping, successes and failures of the League of Nations, the establishment of the United Nations, and post-war cooperation.

topics.title: Depth Study
topics.summary: In-depth study of a specific country, period, or theme such as Germany 1918-1945, the USA 1919-1941, or China 1930-1990.

topics.title: Decolonisation
topics.summary: The end of European empires, independence movements in Africa and Asia, and the challenges faced by newly independent nations.

subjects.name: Geography
subjects.description: Physical and human geography themes
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Geography (0460) develops learners' understanding of physical and human environments, and the interactions between them. The syllabus covers population, settlement, landforms, weather and climate, economic development, and environmental management. Candidates develop map skills, data interpretation, and fieldwork investigation techniques. Assessment includes written papers and a coursework or alternative-to-coursework component. This qualification prepares students for A-Level Geography, environmental science, and urban planning.

topics.title: Population
topics.summary: Population growth, structure, migration patterns, demographic transition model, and population policies including China's one-child policy and pro-natalist policies.

topics.title: Landforms
topics.summary: River processes and landforms, coastal processes and landforms, tectonic processes including volcanoes and earthquakes, and weathering and erosion.

topics.title: Weather & Climate
topics.summary: Climate types, factors affecting climate, weather systems, ecosystems, tropical rainforests, and deserts.

topics.title: Economic Development
topics.summary: Agriculture types, industrial location, tourism, energy resources, trade, and the development gap between countries.

topics.title: Fieldwork
topics.summary: Data collection methods, geographical enquiry, sampling techniques, presentation of data, and analysis and evaluation of fieldwork findings.

topics.title: Settlement
topics.summary: Urban land use models, urbanisation in LEDCs and MEDCs, urban problems and solutions, and sustainable urban living.

subjects.name: Computer Science
subjects.description: Theory, programming, and computational thinking
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Computer Science (0478) develops learners' understanding of computer science principles and programming skills. The syllabus covers data representation, computer architecture, networks, cybersecurity, algorithms, and programming concepts. Candidates learn to write pseudocode and understand high-level programming constructs. Assessment includes theory papers and a practical programming examination. This qualification is essential for A-Level Computer Science, software engineering, and technology careers.

topics.title: Data Representation
topics.summary: Binary, hexadecimal, denary conversions, binary arithmetic, ASCII, Unicode, bitmap and vector images, sound sampling, and data storage units.

topics.title: Hardware & Software
topics.summary: CPU components and architecture, fetch-decode-execute cycle, memory types including RAM and ROM, input and output devices, and operating systems.

topics.title: Networks
topics.summary: Network types including LAN and WAN, topologies, network protocols including TCP/IP, packet switching, network security, and the internet structure.

topics.title: Algorithms
topics.summary: Pseudocode, flowcharts, search algorithms including linear and binary search, sort algorithms including bubble and merge sort, and algorithm efficiency.

topics.title: Programming
topics.summary: Variables, constants, data types, sequence, selection, iteration, arrays, string manipulation, file handling, and structured programming principles.

topics.title: Cybersecurity & Ethics
topics.summary: Malware types, encryption, firewalls, phishing, social engineering, ethical issues, data protection legislation, and digital rights.

subjects.name: French
subjects.description: Foreign language French proficiency
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE French (0520) develops learners' ability to communicate effectively in French across the four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. The syllabus covers everyday topics, grammar, and cultural awareness. Candidates are assessed through listening, reading, speaking, and writing papers, with tiers available for different ability levels. This qualification prepares students for A-Level French, international careers, and further language study.

topics.title: Listening
topics.summary: Understanding spoken French on familiar topics including everyday conversations, announcements, and media extracts. Developing comprehension and inference skills.

topics.title: Reading
topics.summary: Comprehending texts, extracting information, identifying main points and details, and inferring meaning from context across various text types.

topics.title: Speaking
topics.summary: Role-play scenarios, guided conversation, presentation on a chosen topic, and discussion. Developing fluency, pronunciation, and spontaneous communication.

topics.title: Writing
topics.summary: Structured writing tasks including letters, emails, articles, and descriptions with accurate grammar, appropriate register, and coherent organisation.

topics.title: Grammar
topics.summary: Tenses including present, past, future, and conditional; agreement rules; pronouns; prepositions; and complex sentence structures.

topics.title: Culture & Society
topics.summary: French-speaking countries, customs, traditions, education systems, and contemporary issues in the Francophone world.

subjects.name: Spanish
subjects.description: Foreign language Spanish proficiency
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Spanish (0530) develops learners' ability to communicate effectively in Spanish across the four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. The syllabus covers everyday topics, grammar, and cultural awareness of the Spanish-speaking world. Candidates are assessed through listening, reading, speaking, and writing papers, with tiers available for different ability levels. This qualification prepares students for A-Level Spanish, international careers, and further language study.

topics.title: Listening
topics.summary: Understanding spoken Spanish on everyday topics including conversations, announcements, and media. Developing comprehension and inference skills.

topics.title: Reading
topics.summary: Comprehending texts, extracting information, identifying main points and details, and inferring meaning from context across various text types.

topics.title: Speaking
topics.summary: Role-play scenarios, guided conversation, presentation on a chosen topic, and discussion. Developing fluency, pronunciation, and spontaneous communication.

topics.title: Writing
topics.summary: Structured writing tasks including messages, emails, short essays, and descriptions with accurate grammar, appropriate register, and coherent organisation.

topics.title: Grammar
topics.summary: Verb tenses including present, preterite, imperfect, future, and conditional; ser and estar; subjunctive mood; adjective agreement; and pronoun usage.

topics.title: Culture & Society
topics.summary: Spanish-speaking countries, customs, festivals, education systems, and contemporary issues in the Hispanic world.

subjects.name: Additional Mathematics
subjects.description: Advanced functions, calculus, vectors, and trigonometry
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Additional Mathematics (0606) is an advanced qualification designed for high-achieving mathematics students. It extends beyond standard IGCSE Mathematics to cover functions, quadratic functions, polynomials, logarithms, trigonometry, permutations and combinations, binomial series, vectors, and calculus including differentiation and integration. The syllabus assumes prior knowledge of IGCSE Mathematics and is assessed through two papers: Paper 1 (non-calculator) and Paper 2 (calculator). This qualification provides excellent preparation for A-Level Mathematics and Further Mathematics, and is recognised by universities as evidence of strong mathematical ability.

topics.title: Functions
topics.summary: Function notation, domain and range, composite functions, inverse functions, modulus functions, and graph transformations including reflections and translations.

topics.title: Quadratic Functions
topics.summary: Completing the square, maximum and minimum values, discriminant and nature of roots, quadratic inequalities, and solving equations algebraically and graphically.

topics.title: Logarithms & Exponentials
topics.summary: Laws of logarithms, solving exponential equations, natural logarithms ln(x) and e^x, change of base, and asymptotic behaviour of graphs.

topics.title: Trigonometry
topics.summary: Six trigonometric functions, trigonometric identities, solving equations for any magnitude, amplitude and period of functions, and graph sketching.

topics.title: Vectors
topics.summary: Vector notation, addition and subtraction, scalar product, magnitude and direction, position vectors, and geometric applications in two dimensions.

topics.title: Calculus
topics.summary: Differentiation of polynomial, trigonometric, exponential and logarithmic functions, chain rule, product and quotient rules, integration, definite integrals, area under curves, and kinematics applications.

topics.title: Series & Combinations
topics.summary: Binomial expansion for positive integer n, arithmetic and geometric progressions, permutations and combinations, and solving arrangement problems.

subjects.name: Combined Science
subjects.description: Coordinated sciences covering biology, chemistry and physics
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Combined Science (0653) is a single-award qualification that combines biology, chemistry, and physics into one coherent syllabus. It covers core concepts from all three sciences with slightly less depth than the individual sciences, making it ideal for students who want a broad science foundation. The syllabus is aligned with the separate science syllabuses (0610, 0620, 0625) and is assessed through six papers: two per science area. Candidates choose Core or Extended tiers. This qualification provides a foundation for further study but may not meet the entry requirements for some A-Level science courses.

topics.title: Biology
topics.summary: Cell structure, human nutrition, plant nutrition, transport in plants and animals, respiration, coordination, and reproduction. Covers core biological concepts at a foundational level.

topics.title: Chemistry
topics.summary: States of matter, atomic structure, bonding, stoichiometry, acids and bases, salts, electrolysis, metals, and organic chemistry fundamentals.

topics.title: Physics
topics.summary: Motion, forces, energy, waves, light, sound, electricity, magnetism, and thermal physics. Covers essential physical principles without the depth of separate physics.

topics.title: Scientific Skills
topics.summary: Experimental design, data collection, graph plotting, analysis, evaluation, and safety considerations across all three sciences.

topics.title: Earth Science
topics.summary: The Earth and its atmosphere, fossil fuels, climate change, and the water cycle as they relate to biology, chemistry, and physics.

topics.title: Practical Assessment
topics.summary: Practical test skills or alternative to practical questions involving apparatus, measurements, and data analysis across biology, chemistry, and physics.

subjects.name: Environmental Management
subjects.description: Sustainability, ecosystems, pollution, and climate change
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Environmental Management (0680) is a multidisciplinary subject drawing on biology, earth science, geography, economics, and anthropology. It examines sustainable development, the impact of human activity on natural resources, and strategies for environmental sustainability. The syllabus covers rocks and minerals, energy, agriculture, water management, oceans, natural hazards, the atmosphere, human population, and ecosystems. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Theory) and Paper 2 (Management in Context), both source-based. This qualification prepares students for IB Environmental Systems and Society, geography, and environmental science careers.

topics.title: Natural Resources
topics.summary: Formation and extraction of rocks, ores and minerals, sustainable management, energy resources including fossil fuels and renewables, and conservation strategies.

topics.title: Agriculture & Land
topics.summary: Soil composition and properties, types of agriculture, raising yields, environmental impacts of fertilisers and pesticides, soil erosion, and conservation case studies.

topics.title: Water Management
topics.summary: Global water distribution, hydrological cycle, water supply and demand, water quality, pollution sources and control, and water-related diseases.

topics.title: Oceans & Fisheries
topics.summary: Ocean resources, fishing methods, overfishing, marine conservation, quotas, oil spills, MARPOL regulations, and plastic pollution impacts.

topics.title: Natural Hazards
topics.summary: Tectonic, climatic, and biological hazards, risk and vulnerability, prediction, preparation, response, and long-term management strategies for disasters.

topics.title: Atmosphere & Climate
topics.summary: Atmospheric composition, weather and climate basics, air pollution, acid rain, ozone depletion, enhanced greenhouse effect, and climate change mitigation strategies.

topics.title: Population & Ecosystems
topics.summary: Human population growth, structure, migration, carrying capacity, biomes, food chains, biodiversity, conservation, protected areas, and sustainable resource use.

subjects.name: Business Studies
subjects.description: Business activity, marketing, finance, and human resources
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Business Studies (0450) develops learners' understanding of business activity in the public and private sectors, and the importance of innovation and change. The syllabus covers business activity, classification, enterprise, marketing, operations management, finance, human resources, and external influences. Candidates learn to make simple business decisions based on analysis and evaluation. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Short Answer and Data Response) and Paper 2 (Case Study). This qualification provides a foundation for A-Level Business Studies, economics, and management careers.

topics.title: Business Activity
topics.summary: Needs, wants, scarcity, opportunity cost, specialisation, purpose of business, adding value, and classification by sector (primary, secondary, tertiary) and ownership (private vs public).

topics.title: Enterprise & Growth
topics.summary: Entrepreneurship characteristics, business plans, government support, methods of measuring business size, and problems of measuring growth.

topics.title: Marketing
topics.summary: Market research, segmentation, marketing mix (4Ps), product life cycle, pricing strategies, promotion methods, and distribution channels.

topics.title: Operations Management
topics.summary: Production methods (job, batch, flow), quality control, lean production, stock control, economies of scale, and location decisions.

topics.title: Finance
topics.summary: Sources of finance, cash flow forecasting, break-even analysis, income statements, statements of financial position, profitability ratios, and liquidity ratios.

topics.title: Human Resources
topics.summary: Organisational structures, recruitment and selection, training and development, motivation theories, remuneration methods, and industrial relations.

topics.title: External Influences
topics.summary: Government economic policies, legal constraints, environmental and ethical issues, international trade, exchange rates, and globalisation impacts.

subjects.name: Economics
subjects.description: Microeconomics, macroeconomics, and international trade
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Economics (0455) develops understanding of economic terminology and principles, and the ability to apply tools of economic analysis. The syllabus covers the basic economic problem, allocation of resources through market forces, microeconomic decision makers (banks, households, firms), government and the macroeconomy, economic development, and international trade. Candidates learn to work with simple economic data and apply understanding to current issues. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Multiple Choice) and Paper 2 (Structured Questions). This qualification provides a foundation for A-Level Economics, business, and finance careers.

topics.title: Basic Economic Problem
topics.summary: Scarcity, choice, opportunity cost, factors of production, production possibility curves, and the distinction between economic and free goods.

topics.title: Market Forces
topics.summary: Demand and supply curves, equilibrium, price elasticity, income elasticity, cross elasticity, consumer and producer surplus, and market failure.

topics.title: Microeconomic Decision Makers
topics.summary: Role of banks, households as consumers, workers and trade unions, firms, costs of production, revenue, profit, and market structures.

topics.title: Government & Macroeconomy
topics.summary: Macroeconomic aims (growth, low unemployment, low inflation, balance of payments), fiscal, monetary, and supply-side policies, and conflicts between objectives.

topics.title: Economic Development
topics.summary: Population growth, living standards, poverty, income redistribution, and the effects of development on population structure and the environment.

topics.title: International Trade
topics.summary: Specialisation, free trade, protectionism, exchange rates, balance of payments, multinational companies, and globalisation impacts.

topics.title: Economic Indicators
topics.summary: GDP, GNP, inflation measurement, unemployment types, balance of payments accounts, and interpretation of economic data.

subjects.name: Global Perspectives
subjects.description: Research, analysis, evaluation, and collaborative skills
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Global Perspectives (0457) is a skills-based subject that develops critical thinking, research, analysis, evaluation, reflection, communication, and collaboration. Unlike traditional subjects, it does not have a fixed content syllabus; instead, students explore global issues from personal, local, and national perspectives, developing transferable skills. Assessment includes a written examination, individual research report, and team project. This qualification can be used as a qualifying subject for Cambridge ICE and prepares students for Cambridge Global Perspectives & Research at AS/A Level, IB, and university study.

topics.title: Research Skills
topics.summary: Formulating research questions, identifying and evaluating sources, gathering and organising evidence, and conducting literature reviews on global issues.

topics.title: Analysis & Evaluation
topics.summary: Analysing different perspectives, identifying bias and assumptions, evaluating arguments, and weighing evidence to reach informed conclusions.

topics.title: Reflection
topics.summary: Reflecting on personal learning, recognising changes in perspective, identifying strengths and weaknesses, and planning for improvement.

topics.title: Communication
topics.summary: Presenting arguments clearly in writing and speech, using evidence effectively, adapting communication for different audiences, and academic writing conventions.

topics.title: Collaboration
topics.summary: Working effectively in teams, dividing responsibilities, resolving conflicts, building consensus, and managing collaborative projects.

topics.title: Global Issues
topics.summary: Exploring contemporary global challenges such as poverty, conflict, environmental degradation, health, education, and human rights from multiple perspectives.

subjects.name: Art & Design
subjects.description: Fine art, graphic design, photography, and 3D design
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Art & Design (0400) encourages personal expression, imagination, and creativity through a range of artistic media and techniques. The syllabus supports development in fine art, graphic design, photography, textiles, and three-dimensional design. Candidates produce a portfolio of coursework and complete an externally set assignment. Assessment focuses on the development of ideas, exploration of media, recording of observations, and realisation of intentions. This qualification prepares students for A-Level Art and Design, foundation courses, and creative industry careers.

topics.title: Fine Art
topics.summary: Drawing, painting, mixed media, printmaking, and sculpture. Developing observational skills, personal expression, and understanding of historical and contemporary art practice.

topics.title: Graphic Design
topics.summary: Typography, layout, illustration, branding, poster design, and digital media. Understanding visual communication and the design process from brief to solution.

topics.title: Photography
topics.summary: Digital and darkroom techniques, composition, lighting, portraiture, landscape, and documentary photography. Developing a personal photographic vision.

topics.title: Textiles
topics.summary: Fabric manipulation, printing, weaving, embroidery, fashion design, and textile art. Exploring material qualities and surface decoration.

topics.title: 3D Design
topics.summary: Sculpture, ceramics, product design, architectural models, and installation art. Working with form, space, structure, and material properties in three dimensions.

topics.title: Critical Studies
topics.summary: Researching artists and designers, analysing visual work, understanding cultural contexts, and developing the ability to articulate artistic intentions and evaluate outcomes.

subjects.name: Music
subjects.description: Performance, composition, listening, and world music
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Music (0410) is the only major IGCSE Music qualification available, offered by Cambridge Assessment International Education. It develops skills in listening, performing, and composing across Western classical traditions and world music. The syllabus covers Baroque, Classical, Romantic, and 20th-century Western music, plus an annual World Focus on non-Western traditions. Assessment includes Component 1 (Listening Exam, 40%), Component 2 (Performing, 30%), and Component 3 (Composing, 30%). This qualification prepares students for A-Level Music, music conservatoires, and professional music careers.

topics.title: Western Classical Tradition
topics.summary: Baroque (1600-1750), Classical (1750-1820), Romantic (1820-1900), and 20th Century music. Stylistic characteristics, set works, and analysis of orchestral, chamber, and vocal repertoire.

topics.title: World Music
topics.summary: Annual World Focus region studying non-Western traditions such as Indian, Caribbean, Arab, Chinese, or African music. Instruments, rhythmic and melodic systems, and cultural contexts.

topics.title: Listening Skills
topics.summary: Identifying melody, harmony, rhythm, texture, structure, instrumentation, dynamics, and articulation. General listening skills and aural recognition.

topics.title: Performance
topics.summary: Solo performance (one piece, 3-4 minutes) and ensemble performance (one piece, 3-4 minutes). Technical accuracy, fluency, interpretation, and musical communication.

topics.title: Composition
topics.summary: Free composition (any style, minimum 1 minute) and composition to a brief (Cambridge-set parameters, minimum 1 minute). Notation, creativity, and structural coherence.

topics.title: Set Works
topics.summary: Detailed study of two prescribed works per exam session, covering structure, themes, keys, chords, and instruments. Deep analysis and contextual understanding.

subjects.name: Drama
subjects.description: Devising, text performance, and theatre theory
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Drama (0411) is the only IGCSE Drama qualification available. It develops practical skills in performance, devising, and theoretical understanding of theatre. Candidates work on extracts from published plays, devise original drama from stimuli, and develop acting, directing, and design skills. The syllabus covers performance skills, staging and design, dramatic terminology, and the process of moving from script to performance. Assessment includes Component 1 (Written Examination, 40%) and Component 2 (Devised Performance and Performance, 60%). This qualification prepares students for A-Level Drama, theatre studies, and performance careers.

topics.title: Script to Performance
topics.summary: Working with extracts from published plays, interpreting text, bringing scripts alive through voice, movement, timing, and understanding the relationship between spoken word and physical expression.

topics.title: Devising
topics.summary: Creating original drama from stimuli such as poems, images, songs, or historical events. Developing scenarios, characters, structure, and evaluating the devising process.

topics.title: Performance Skills
topics.summary: Vocal techniques (articulation, projection, inflection), physical skills (movement, gesture, posture), character creation, and ensemble work.

topics.title: Staging & Design
topics.summary: Set, props, lighting, sound, costume, and make-up contributions to performance. Understanding stage types (proscenium, thrust, theatre-in-the-round) and spatial awareness.

topics.title: Directing
topics.summary: Advising actors, developing directorial concepts, creating mood and atmosphere, using space and levels, and communicating with the audience.

topics.title: Dramatic Terminology
topics.summary: Core terms including action, chorus, comedy, tragedy, conflict, tension, exposition, climax, resolution, blocking, improvisation, and proxemics.

subjects.name: Physical Education
subjects.description: Anatomy, physiology, skill acquisition, and sport psychology
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Physical Education (0413) combines theoretical knowledge of sports science with practical performance in physical activities. The syllabus covers anatomy and physiology, health and fitness, skill acquisition, sports psychology, and social, cultural, and ethical influences on sport. Candidates also undertake four practical activities from at least two of seven categories. Assessment is 50% theory (Paper 1) and 50% coursework (practical performance). This qualification prepares students for A-Level PE, IB Sport Science, and careers in sports coaching, therapy, and management.

topics.title: Anatomy & Physiology
topics.summary: Skeletal and muscular systems, joint types and movements, respiratory system, circulatory system, aerobic and anaerobic respiration, and effects of exercise on the body.

topics.title: Health & Fitness
topics.summary: Components of fitness, fitness testing, principles of training, training methods, diet and energy, warming up and cooling down, and injury prevention.

topics.title: Skill Acquisition
topics.summary: Skill classification (open/closed, basic/complex), information processing model, stages of learning (cognitive, associative, autonomous), feedback types, and guidance methods.

topics.title: Sports Psychology
topics.summary: Motivation, arousal, anxiety, goal setting, mental preparation, relaxation techniques, personality types, and concentration strategies.

topics.title: Social & Cultural Influences
topics.summary: Leisure and recreation, sports development pyramid, participation trends, sponsorship, media, global events, professional vs amateur sport, and technology in sport.

topics.title: Ethics & Sport
topics.summary: Performance-enhancing drugs, blood doping, risk assessment, injuries, sportsmanship, fair play, and discrimination in sport.

topics.title: Practical Performance
topics.summary: Four activities from at least two categories (games, gymnastics, dance, athletics, outdoor, swimming, combat). Skill execution, tactics, and effectiveness in performance.

subjects.name: Religious Studies
subjects.description: Beliefs, teachings, practices, and ethics of world religions
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Religious Studies (0490) develops learners' understanding of religious beliefs, practices, and ethical issues. The syllabus focuses on Christianity and Islam (with Judaism as an option), covering worship, beliefs, scriptures, sacred places, festivals, rites of passage, family life, and social action. Candidates develop skills in constructing well-argued, informed, and balanced written arguments. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Worship, Beliefs, Scriptures, Sacred Places) and Paper 2 (Festivals, Family, Social Action). This qualification prepares students for A-Level Religious Studies, philosophy, ethics, and careers in law, social work, and education.

topics.title: Beliefs & Teachings
topics.summary: Core beliefs about God, creation, the afterlife, prophets, and holy books in Christianity and Islam. Understanding creeds, articles of faith, and theological concepts.

topics.title: Worship & Practices
topics.summary: Forms of worship, prayer, sacraments, pilgrimage, and religious ceremonies. The role of the church, mosque, and synagogue in community life.

topics.title: Sacred Texts
topics.summary: The Bible, Qur'an, and Torah. Authority, interpretation, key stories, teachings, and the role of scripture in guiding moral and religious life.

topics.title: Festivals & Rites of Passage
topics.summary: Religious festivals including Christmas, Easter, Eid al-Fitr, Eid al-Adha. Birth, marriage, and funeral ceremonies and their significance.

topics.title: Ethics & Social Action
topics.summary: Moral codes, ethical decision-making, stewardship of the environment, poverty relief, charity work, and the role of religious organisations in social justice.

topics.title: Family & Society
topics.summary: Religious teachings on family roles, marriage, divorce, gender equality, tradition and change, and the challenges of modern society for religious communities.

subjects.name: Travel & Tourism
subjects.description: Tourism industry, destinations, customer service, and marketing
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Travel & Tourism (0471) provides understanding of the global travel and tourism industry, its structure, and the factors affecting its development. The syllabus covers tourism definitions and concepts, global tourism, travel and tourism organisations, customer service, and destination marketing. Candidates learn about sustainable tourism, economic impacts, and the skills required for employment in the industry. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Key Terms and Concepts) and Paper 2 (Managing and Marketing Destinations). This qualification prepares students for further study in tourism management, hospitality, and related service industries.

topics.title: Tourism Concepts
topics.summary: Definitions of tourism, types of tourism (leisure, business, adventure), reasons for travel, characteristics of tourism, and types of tourists and destinations.

topics.title: Global Tourism
topics.summary: Scale of global tourism, features of destinations, appeal to different tourist types, accessibility, infrastructure, and sustainable tourism development.

topics.title: Tourism Organisations
topics.summary: Role of national tourism organisations, destination management companies, airlines, hotels, and tour operators. Partnerships and sustainable practices.

topics.title: Customer Service
topics.summary: Importance of customer service, handling complaints and enquiries, making reservations, product knowledge, and skills required for tourism employment.

topics.title: Destination Marketing
topics.summary: Marketing mix (product, price, place, promotion), market research, segmentation, targeting, branding, and distribution channels for tourism products.

topics.title: Sustainable Tourism
topics.summary: Environmental, economic, and sociocultural impacts of tourism, carrying capacity, overtourism, seasonality, and strategies for responsible tourism management.

subjects.name: Information & Communication Technology
subjects.description: Hardware, software, networks, data, and multimedia applications
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE ICT (0417) develops practical skills in using software applications and theoretical understanding of ICT systems. The syllabus covers hardware, software, networks, data types, the impact of ICT on society, and systems analysis. Practical skills include document production, data manipulation, spreadsheets, databases, presentations, data analysis, and website authoring. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Theory, 40%), Paper 2 (Document Production, Data Manipulation, Presentations, 30%), and Paper 3 (Data Analysis and Website Authoring, 30%). This qualification prepares students for A-Level IT, computer science, and digital careers.

topics.title: Hardware & Software
topics.summary: Types and components of computer systems, input and output devices, storage devices and media, system software, application software, and utility programs.

topics.title: Networks
topics.summary: Network types (LAN, WAN), network topologies, internet structure, protocols, network security, and the role of network hardware including routers and switches.

topics.title: Data & Security
topics.summary: Data types, data integrity, validation and verification, malware, encryption, firewalls, phishing, data protection legislation, and safe internet practices.

topics.title: Document Production
topics.summary: Word processing skills including formatting, mail merge, tables, styles, and document design for professional presentation.

topics.title: Spreadsheets & Databases
topics.summary: Formulas, functions, charts, sorting, filtering in spreadsheets. Database design, tables, relationships, queries, forms, and reports.

topics.title: Website & Presentation
topics.summary: HTML and CSS authoring, website structure, navigation, and design. Presentation software including transitions, animations, and multimedia integration.

topics.title: Impact of ICT
topics.summary: Social, economic, and ethical effects of ICT on individuals, organisations, and society. Emerging technologies, automation, and digital divides.

subjects.name: Design & Technology
subjects.description: Product design, resistant materials, systems, and graphics
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Design & Technology (0445) develops creative thinking and problem-solving skills through designing and making products. The syllabus includes common content in product design and a specialist option in Resistant Materials, Systems & Control, or Graphic Products. Candidates learn the design process, materials and manufacturing, technical drawing, CAD/CAM, and sustainability. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Product Design, 25%), Paper 2-4 (Specialist Option, 25%), and a Design Project (50%). This qualification prepares students for A-Level Design & Technology, engineering, and creative careers.

topics.title: Product Design
topics.summary: Design briefs and specifications, research methods, idea generation, design development, evaluation, and the iterative design process.

topics.title: Resistant Materials
topics.summary: Properties and applications of woods, metals, plastics, and composites. Preparation, shaping, joining, and finishing techniques.

topics.title: Systems & Control
topics.summary: Mechanisms, structures, electronics, and forces. Understanding systems, control inputs and outputs, and programmable components.

topics.title: Graphic Products
topics.summary: Formal drawing techniques, pictorial and orthographic views, CAD software, presenting and communicating design information, and modelling.

topics.title: CAD/CAM
topics.summary: Computer-aided design for modelling and prototyping, computer-aided manufacture including laser cutting and 3D printing, and digital technologies in industry.

topics.title: Sustainability & Ethics
topics.summary: Sustainable design principles, material selection for environmental impact, recycling, ethical manufacturing, and the social impact of design.

topics.title: Design Project
topics.summary: Independent design-and-make project from brief to finished product, including research, design folder, prototyping, manufacturing, and evaluation.

subjects.name: Accounting
subjects.description: Bookkeeping, financial statements, and business analysis
subjects.summary: Cambridge IGCSE Accounting (0452) introduces the theory and concepts of accounting and develops practical skills in preparing and interpreting financial information. The syllabus covers the fundamentals of accounting, sources and recording of data, verification of accounting records, accounting procedures, preparation of financial statements for various entities, analysis and interpretation, and accounting principles and policies. Assessment includes Paper 1 (Multiple Choice, 30%) and Paper 2 (Structured Questions, 70%). This qualification provides a foundation for A-Level Accounting, business, finance, and professional accountancy careers.

topics.title: Fundamentals of Accounting
topics.summary: The purpose of accounting, the accounting equation, double-entry bookkeeping, and the distinction between assets, liabilities, income, and expenses.

topics.title: Sources & Recording of Data
topics.summary: Business documents, books of prime entry including sales and purchases day books, cash book, petty cash book, and the general journal.

topics.title: Verification of Records
topics.summary: Trial balance, types of errors, correction of errors, bank reconciliation statements, and control accounts for sales and purchases ledgers.

topics.title: Accounting Procedures
topics.summary: Capital vs revenue expenditure, depreciation methods, irrecoverable debts, allowance for doubtful debts, inventory valuation, and accruals and prepayments.

topics.title: Financial Statements
topics.summary: Income statements and statements of financial position for sole traders, partnerships, limited companies, manufacturing businesses, clubs, and incomplete records.

topics.title: Analysis & Interpretation
topics.summary: Accounting ratios (profitability, liquidity, efficiency), interpretation of financial performance, inter-business comparison, and limitations of accounting statements.

topics.title: Principles & Policies
topics.summary: Accounting concepts including prudence, consistency, accruals, going concern, materiality, and the impact of ethical considerations and technology on accounting.
